title: Why Batches Need Equal Lengths
concept: GPUs process data in rectangular matrices, so sentences in a batch must be padded with a dummy token to match the longest sequence.

exercises.prompt: What shape does a GPU prefer when processing batches of data?
exercises.explain: GPUs are designed to perform operations on structured grids of data, meaning everything in a batch must share the same shape.

cards.title: The Neat-Freak GPU
cards.body: Have you ever wondered how computers process millions of words at the same time? They do it by bundling sentences together. But there is a catch: computers are obsessed with neat grids, and sentences are almost never the same length!

cards.title: What is Batch Padding?
cards.body: A GPU is a super-fast calculator that loves neat, rectangular grids of numbers (matrices). Because text sentences naturally have different lengths, we must pad shorter sentences with special dummy tokens so they all match the length of the longest sentence in the batch.

cards.title: The Lunchbox Fit
cards.body: Imagine packaging sandwiches for a lunchbox. If you have long baguettes and short sliders, packing them side-by-side in a tight, square container is tough. To keep the box neat and prevent things from shifting, you might wrap the shorter sliders in extra bubble wrap (padding) until they are just as long as the baguettes, making a perfect square block.

cards.title: Padding in Action
cards.body:
Let's say we have three sentences:
1. "I love AI" (3 words)
2. "Deep learning is awesome" (4 words)
3. "Yes" (1 word)

To batch them, we find the longest one (4 words) and pad the rest:
• "I love AI [PAD]"
• "Deep learning is awesome"
• "Yes [PAD] [PAD] [PAD]"

cards.title: Do Dummy Words Confuse the AI?
cards.body: You might think padding tokens will confuse the AI and make it think the dummy words are important. Don't worry! AI models use special "attention masks" to ignore the padding tokens completely during calculation.

cards.title: Visualizing Batch Shapes
cards.body: Without padding, our sentences form an uneven shape that a GPU cannot process. With padding, we get a neat, uniform rectangle.

exercises.prompt: Why does a GPU require sentences in a batch to be the same length?

options.text: GPUs only understand words that are five letters long.
GPUs process data in rectangular grids, requiring all rows to have the same length.
Short sentences make the GPU overheat and slow down.
Padding tokens store the actual meaning of the sentences.

exercises.explain: GPUs process data in rigid, rectangular grids (matrices). If the rows of data have different lengths, the GPU cannot calculate them efficiently.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Padding tokens are added to give the AI new information and help it understand the vocabulary better.
exercises.explain: Padding tokens (like [PAD]) are just placeholders to fill space; they do not carry any meaning and are ignored during core calculations.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the rule of batch padding.
exercises.sentence: When batching sentences, we must pad all of them to match the length of the _______ sentence.
exercises.explain: To create a neat grid, we must pad all shorter sentences to match the exact length of the longest sentence in the batch.

exercises.prompt: An AI assistant is trying to pad the sentences "Hello AI" and "Welcome to learning" into a batch. Spot the invalid batch that has NOT been padded correctly.
exercises.explain: The third option is generated by an AI that doesn't understand padding. It just repeated the words without adding dummy tokens to make the lengths equal.

exercises.prompt: Why do we use padding tokens when preparing batches of sentences for a GPU?

options.text: They make all sentences in a batch match the length of the longest sentence, creating a perfect rectangular grid for the GPU.
They erase extra words from long sentences to make them as short as the shortest sentence.
They translate sentences into different languages to match word counts.
They speed up the network by randomly deleting words.

exercises.explain: To process data in batches, the computer must arrange it into a clean, rectangular grid. Since sentences naturally have different word counts, we add padding tokens to make them all match the longest sentence in the batch.
